item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article has been translated into Spanish
http://lafundacionscp.wikidot.com/scp-2472

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great paradox. If it's nonanomalous, but it's triggering our detectors, then triggering our detectors is its anomalous property. So it's an anomaly.

But if it's an anomaly and it triggers our detectors, then that's not an anomalous property, and it's an otherwise mundane object. So it has no anomalous property. So it's not an anomaly.

But it's triggering our detec……hahahaha

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this.

First, the idea of having anomaly detection software and sort of going through Foundation guidelines on identifying the extranormal is itself interesting. But what really gets me about this piece is the meta-anomalous aspect.

The whole idea of the anomaly is something that can't be understood, despite our best efforts. While a lot of the appeal of the Foundation is that it takes modern scientific processes to deal with the paranormal, and a lot of paratech/lore has been developed to understand and classify anomalies. Humes, thaumaturgy, cognitohazards, etc. all exist to better bucket the inexplicable into something more explicable.

This turns the whole idea on its head. We create software systems to detect anomalies, by looking for sets of patterns and then seeing what matches known anomalies. Or just measuring the thing and seeing where it doesn't fit.

For whatever reason, this thing is a statistical anomaly in how we measure anomalies. Whether it's just a one in a trillion or actually paranormal is left as an exercise to the reader.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did not find the idea particularly intriguing, and I guess that I lack the specific context, as the general context of "hyacinth = southern witches" was nowhere near enough to maintain my interest. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely interesting.
Particularly, the GOI's anomaly detector section has made me thrilled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess the reason this doesn't work for me is that the conclusion drawn at the end doesn't make sense. Dr. Researcher concludes that if is is possible for something non-anomalous to appear anomalous, it is possible for something anomalous to appear non-anomalous. The problem is that… That's not true.

The air coupler is anomalous. Pretty much undeniably. It anomalously is described as a dangerous anomaly. A thing that is anomalous is ALSO anomalous. It's not that something non-anomalous is described as anomalous, because it is anomalous. Its anomaly is that it has an anomaly, despite that anomaly not doing anything. If an SRA will accurately determine that an anomaly is anomalous, then by firing when introduced to this object, it has done its job and detected an anomaly. Not firing in the case of something that is an anomaly would be different.

Suppose that I built a machine that would look through postcards, and would alert me if any of them had a fact about the city of Boston. And it binged on something that said "The City of Boston is described by the text on this card." And so it binged that as describing a new fact about Boston. Which it was! Even though no INTERESTING information about Boston was mentioned on the card, nothing about the streets or buildings or history or what have you, it still did describe it. It just described it as being described. And it did so accurately.

It would not follow that, because you could make it fire for a fact that didn't describe what I wanted to know, you would be able to make it not fire if you described Boston as being the location of Fenway Park, something that IS what I want to know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has anyone made the comparison between 2472 and the 'bald furby' from 'Black books' yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wtf did I get affected by the article while tagging this

Now I'm conflicted on fixing that and changing it back to Safe. Cus it probably should be safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I the only one that notices that the tags actually say this is keter?

That's cosmic level mindscrew, Kate is a genius writer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is the concept tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are a number of articles like this- they're usually called Semantic anomalies. (I think there at least used to be a tag for them, but maybe I'm wrong, because there isn't one now and there'd be no reason to remove it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've had a notion that might make this part of an interesting canon.

Taking the Platonic idea of thingness - the essence of what a thing is, that is distinct from the thing itself - what if a someone has created a device that can detach and re-attach the thingness of things?

What if some organization has managed to swap the non-anomalous thingness of an air coupler with the highly anomalous thingness of a Keter object?

Oh, the places we could go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 For the sheer amount of doubt about wether the Foundation is actually able to contain, let alone find, the anomalies that need to be contained the most

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And almost two years after the fact, I finally find a chance to read it (and find it, wikidot really is horribly designed). If I recall the point of my suggestion correctly, this was precisely the direction I was hoping this would go. I really do love articles that carry on a bit about Foundation developed technology.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wait

What if this thing alters probability randomly?

But, it only happened to alter probability when it comes to detecting anomalies, and no other time, just by the pure one-in-a-trigintillion chance, which wasn't a coincidence, because it can alter probability to make it seem like an incredibly unlikely coincidence took place.

Maybe the reality-distortion inside of SCP-2472 really wants to distort reality, but it doesn't care how, so it uses probability alteration to distort reality in the one way it can without being noticed by the Foundation? Maybe all anomalous things just have a strong desire to divert from what we have set as the norm for what is reality? (What if gods from another reality sent us these things to show us a message that we could develop the ability to control reality, and that the universe has no limits, under the assumption that humanity would be advanced enough to not be bothered by things like SCP-682, and that we'd simply see it as a hint that nothing is impossible?)

What if 2472 is a god, and it's trying to tell the Foundation, humanity, and our universe as a whole that reality alteration can be kept under control, and used to our advantage? What if we're just not as far along other universes progress-wise, and they throw anomalies at us as a sort of "training"? That's why it alters probability when it is tested as an anomaly?

Because, they'll keep testing the thing, and it uses probability alteration to make the tests just so happen to malfunction or something, making the Foundation think that the anomaly is that the anomaly isn't anomalous, but is?

+1, I need to lie down…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing is scarier.
I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't yet read most of the stuff connected to this, but it worked for me quite well; the characterization at the end seemed slightly on those nose, but otherwise, I liked this.

Bit confused by all the claims, largely by people who do have the context that I don't, that it doesn't stand on its own without that context. Curious. But I tend to see a lot of those sorts of comments on Kate's work, so maybe it's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DNM and the greater Gulf canon are about giving a slice of life/grounds eye view of the lives of Foundation personnel. What I'm imagining would be more a metatextual story that would mirror what is happening with the SCP wiki's own community, with an eye towards allow a new reader to get the same thrill that the people who liked this got when they read it.

As for your arguments against SRA tech: pretty close to my own views. Only thing I would add is that if the Foundation understood what was going on well enough to create this sort of tech, then they wouldn't need to contain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short version: I don't really think this stands on its own, build a canon to support it.

Isn't it already part of Dixieland Nightmare Magic?

Hume units and Scranton reality boxes

+ sirpudding's rant about SRA tech
- They said I was mad at the University! Mad!

I think readily available SRAs (and related tech like Kant meters and the Humes they measure) aren't really very good narrative elements for what we do here (if they are mass produced; one-off "black boxes" are okay). As far as I can tell the SRA was first introduced in SCP-1451 as "black box" tech from an alternate universe Foundation, in order to allow some information transfer between timelines. Which is fine. As reproducible technology though, it's a problem IMO for three reasons:

  • It is technobabble. Nobody knows how these things work or what the limits are. It replaces creativity in containment procedures with a black box.
  • If actually used widely it would make containment of the vast majority of skips trivial. Most skips that are hard to contain, are hard to contain because of aphysical properties. So if you have a magic box that (ironically) "makes science work" in it's field of effect, you stop anything that violates causality, thermodynamics, relativity and/or conservation. Which is the vast majority of Eucild and Keter skips. Which makes this whole thing a lot less interesting.
  • As you say, it's a lot of extraneous background that either needs to be explained each time, or used in such a way that the audience just assimilates it as more weirdness.

That said, I like the hints of a covert organization with institutional paranoia, moles, and witch-hunts we get here. I'd like to see more of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short version: I don't really think this stands on its own, build a canon to support it.

Long version: So, coming to this a bit late (roughly half a year), I think that my problem with this is a matter of context.
I've been a long time lurker, I came in sometime after grandiose decommission stories were on the way out, but before the final nail was driven into the telekil coffin. I followed the scp-2000 contest eagerly, and as such am familiar with the origins of Hume units and Scranton reality boxes and the stuff PeppersGhost referred to as "Science of Anomalies", so I have the context needed to appreciate VAElynx's and TL333s's reactions.
However, take it from the perspective of a newcomer. Someone who got linked to, say 682, 914, 1111 and then this. This reader would not have the context to get a really strong reaction either way (appreciation for undermining a potentially weak element, dislike of use of said element). To this reader, it would be pretty meh.

To my mind, the best solution would be for to turn this into the capstone of a canon that shows the invention of a cool new toy (science of anomalies), the excitement it caused, its proliferation, its misuse, and ultimately its downfall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The difference is that this one makes the automatic detection equipment " see " an anomaly in something that is not anomalous according to people, while sc- 014-j makes people see an anomaly where the equipment and others say none exists.

The other complaint that keeps popping up is that the foundation can't automate the classification process. This isn't as hard as you'd expect, as evidenced by the strides face recognition tech has made. But, just like frt, it's far from perfect…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea that this was actively created by some junior guy who went, "You know what? Automating the process of detecting anomalies means it's looking for specific traits, so let's make something with those traits but no actual anomalies," just to try and break down a crappy system.

Then he saw them continue to be used, and decided to make something anomalous that couldn't be automatically detected, just for giggles and to prove his point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the main idea about the article, but there are two things that bother me too much to upvote.

The first thing is the fact that the Foundation has an object class detector. I'm ok with humes and kant counters, but having a machine that detects object class? That just iffy. First of all, keter doesn't mean dangerous (which is the vibe I got from this article) but just hard to contain. Object class is very situational, what was keter ten years ago, could be safe today. It would work if the machine was a really impressive AI that would take into account all the personnel, materials, and budget the site has, and then use that to evaluate how difficult it would be to contain the SCP.

The second thing is the last footnote. It not only gives away something that could have been left as fridge horror for the reader to figure out, but it is also weirdly place. If that note is so important to the article and the point its trying to make, why is it just placed there at the end where it could be easily missed? Seems like something that important would be its own addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, let me get this straight; this thing is totally non-anomalous, except for that it's detected as an anomaly, which is it's only anomalous trait?

Well, that's ironic. Sort of. Not really, but it's almost as if it deserves a word with a meaning close to ironic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my first post, and it's to say I'm downvoting. :(

This is basically http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-014-j in a different form, and features a general-use analysis system that exists solely to be defeated as it's *primary* element. Can't get behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, now we can say we have one.

This is about as well-executed as that particular idea can be executed, which is why I'm neutral voting rather than downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am aware of this. I was implying that this makes me think of AWCY?, which I consider a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not there. Look at how the note ends and cross reference it with Kate's others Dixieland Nightmare Magic skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think part of my concern is that this doesn't strike me as a terribly interesting way — "What if they don't work??" — and that there might not be much in the way of more interesting ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's still a 2572 in the Containment Procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any system which can be built can be countered, exploited, and broken.

I'm still trying to find the "Are we cool yet?" that has got to be hidden in there somewhere.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted, but I highly doubt that the Foundation would have a technology that could determine an object's class.

Remember: the class is generally determined by ease of containment, not by how hazardous the object is.

Kate, it kinda looks like you fell into the newbie trap of "Keter=Very Dangerous". Unless, of course, the whole point of my post was the exact reason of you putting it in: showing that it can't be done by showing what happens if object classification is attempted by automated technology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting food for thought à la 1609, but the note at the end makes me feel like I'm not getting the whole picture. No vote for now. Once I get more acquainted with your canon, then I might feel more comfortable voting one way or the other…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The relatively recent addition of Humes, Scrantons, Kants, and the Science of Anomalies in general, to the SCP Universe is something I've been on the fence about for a while now. However, after reading this article, I've decided that I can get on board with those concepts as long as they're used in interesting ways. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I highly approve of this.

Thank you for deconstructing the ridiculous notion that the Foundation can automagically detect anomalies. I even like the inter-Foundation publications, because they help sell the utter impossibility of that concept. +1

EDIT: Not to mention the further real-world implications, the critique of needless reliance on technology where humanity has always sufficed. So, not Luddite, but common sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. An item that makes the researchers go crazy trying to figure out what it does, when it does… NOTHING. LOL This is really skirting the line of being a -J though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but the idea that there are basically Foundation sonic screwdrivers that can, for example, tell if something should be Keter seems…too easy.

That's the thing. They can't. We thought they can, but we have just hit what looks like the first brick wall with them.
It's precisely why I like the article - it kinda outlines that there's a limit to what looked like a mary-sue make-believe technology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate this comment, as always. Your feedback continues to inspire me to put out my best work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially +1'd, and then after a few minutes, reverted to a no-vote. I like what's going on, and the idea that this little widget is causing so much confusion and strain on Foundation personnel, but the idea that there are basically Foundation sonic screwdrivers that can, for example, tell if something should be Keter seems…too easy. That should be a human logic call, and not inherent in the object like chemical composition. I don't like it when things are easy for the Foundation (which is one reason I responded so positively to "We Who Poke With Sticks"), and all these automatic whatzits inherent to the story do just that.

I did think it was well-written and enjoyed the entry otherwise, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is probably one of the more reluctant downvotes I've registered recently. It comes down, eventually, to a matter of personal philosophy.

This has a lot going for it in terms of prompts for engaging the imagination of the reader. Without clubbing the reader over the head with a Dr. Author insert going "my God man, do you know what this MEANS?", you've introduced a nice element of doubt about the very nature of what the Foundation is doing, and the way in which this was written very subtly displays a person writing this up by the numbers while thinking "oh fuck oh fuck oh fuck" the entire time. There is also a lot of worldbuilding going on, and the fact that the Swiss government is addressing anomalous phenomena on its own without an intermediary agency made me really want to support this.

Also, I must extend my gratitude for not just simply pointing a fucking Kant counter at this.

My stumbling block is more personal than anything else, and I'm afraid there's not a whole lot to be done about on your end, and that's the principle that an article must be able to stand on its own on some level. That's not to say that individual works cannot be enhanced by subsequent efforts, or that further context can't be generated after the fact. But when it comes to the final vote, there is a personal threshold for how much intrinsic story a piece contains that has to be met for me to feel comfortable supporting it. I'm frustrated that I can't more precisely articulate this threshold, because I want well-written things to survive, and this is well-written.

This takes place within a larger context of how to grapple with this large-scale format of storytelling that you've employed, and like other pieces within that larger framework I will probably revisit this as I form a more cohesive vision of how to deal with DNM as a whole. At this time, my response to this is to congratulate you on meeting the usual high standard you've set for yourself and for seeding the elements of a larger narrative, but to somewhat regretfully downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the paranoia potential for this one, but the idea that there are devices in the SCP world that are able to test objects for anomalousness is a bit too much for my headcanon to accept.
Part of the appeal of the whole SCP shtick for me is precisely that we can never know for sure if something is anomalous or not and how dangerous it could be, so being able to wave your tricorder around to detect a Keter in the next room kills a lot of this suspense.
So having a SCP built upon the existence and reliability of these devices really does not work for me, even if it is precisely proving them to be unreliable.

No-voting because my headcanon is the one at fault here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "An air coupler, used for air coupling"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. Like Dr Nietzsche above, I can see several options for this, including the ones he mentioned, but also a fourth option (to my mind the most boring):

This is anomalous. The anomaly is something incredibly subtle which doesn't make any difference in a practical sense and is essentially undetectable - maybe there's a carbon atom in there with five covalent bonds instead of four, for example - which is theoretically Keter since it means that the chemistry and therefore the physics underlying the object is incompatible with the real-world physics, and it's possible it happens to be in a semi-stable state at the moment which could be tipped over at any moment with potentially catastrophic results… But in practical terms this is so unlikely that it's not a realistic concern, so this trips all the alarms while being to all intents and purposes entirely mundane. The biggest weakness of this idea, in my mind, is that if this is the case it allows for "false" positives but not false negatives, which means there's no real possibility for fridge horror.

For the record, I actually like the third option you mentioned. It's a basic rule of scientific testing that there is essentially always a possibility of false positives and false negatives, and making tests more rigorous in avoiding one increases the probability of the other. Some tests are incredibly reliable, such as pH testing, but I imagine that anomaly testing is less reliable than that. If the third option is the case, the item itself is not particularly interesting, but it's a nice piece of worldbuilding that acknowledges the weakness of even the Foundation's methods.

I also like the first one, because it's basically an item where its anomaly is that it's anomalous while not having any anomalous properties whatsoever - it's an elegant paradox.

For the record, this seems to be essentially the same concept as the former Foundation member targeted by a GOI and marked as an SCP in response - it's just that this time, the anomaly detection is tech rather than a GOI. A very different take on fundamentally the same idea.

It's certainly interesting enough to view in different lights, and it gets better the more you think about it. Definite +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what?
I love this, and if I could upvote twice, I would.

This article, together with the rest of Kate's work actually estabilishes something sensible and intriguing going on with the whole Scranton reality anchor business , and making me hate it less.

Basically, it's almost like with every cookie-cutter invention out there. First, once it was arrived to, it seemed like a panacea, doing a good deal of work for the Foundation staff, and leading to vast speculation about unified theories of anomalies and other such bull, automated recognition systems and whatnot.
Except then it started falling down like a house of cards, and this was perhaps the first swallow.

An object which seems to lack anomalous properties except for consistently triggering all these technologies. Whether it's naturally occurring or deliberate, its impact on theory of anomalous objects is the same as Turing's Halting Problem had on computing - it delineated that we can't rely on Scranton technology to do our work for us.

The fact that a researcher seems to have figured out a way to go at it the other way, and disappeared to carry out whatever, clearly involving the Order of the Hyacinth is just a bonus.

Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Swiss

Yep. They're going from Banking to Containment. We are out of a business.

In any case, frankly, I'm gonna no-vote simply due to the "Binah Pattern Recognition System". See, if a pattern recognition system recognizes something is very wrong with an analyzed item, it means its programmers / makers have thought of every percievable and possible pattern that can be analyzed when programming / making it… so why can't the makers perceive the anomaly?

Because, at its core, it's a Thing What Makes You Not Notice It, at the expense of being notable to pretty much everything else.

Sure, it's very well handled, but perception filters are barely interesting for me. Sorry, Kate. I'll re-read later (I'm a bit too asleep at this point, tbh) and look at it with new eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like the end footnote hints at, it is not about making something mundane that sets off every alarm, it is the possibility to do the opposite; that you can make something dangerous that can avoid every alarm that makes this article truly interesting/horrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
None of those concepts really appeal to me. Again, caveat here that it is late and I am pretty tired, but this doesn't really spark my interest much. It's definitely food for thought, but it wasn't that enjoyable so far as an SCP goes.

I might take another look at this tomorrow, but I'm not overly inclined to change my vote as of now. I'll keep your comment in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hum, I disagree. I find it incredibly interesting, if only because there's three likely possibilities. Two of them are interesting, one of them isn't.

One - The only anomalous property is the fact that people who know what they're looking for find it super-anomalous, and that it was designed that way as both proof of concept and bait. They've made something utterly harmless that all our tech and specialists seem to think is Keter-level bad. This means that it's possible to, as the footnotes state, to make something extremely, badly anomalous and we just accept that it's normal, so we don't question. We don't even know to ask. We won't know. And we'll never know if they're about to do it. Or if that it has already been done and we're just slow on the uptake to find this trinket.

Two(my faveorite) - There is something keter-level bad about this. But we are completely unable to see it. We're powerless against it. And we'll never know how badly we've failed.

Three (the Boring Option!) - It's exactly what it says it is, and it's setting off our equipment and screwing with our scientists because if there was NOT an object that would by sheer chance do that, it could potentially mean some incredibly, even-worse-bad metaphysical things regarding the Foundationverse as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with this. Granted, I'm not overly familiar with a substantial amount of the terminology/lingo used here, I think, so that might be part of the issue for me.

It just seems a bit too bland. Maybe it would make a lot of sense in-universe to have as just something floating around as an example of this sort of thing, but I can't say I enjoyed this much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As usual, well-written, but ultimately it fails to interest me in any way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
..

This is fucking horrifying.

All of my +1. All of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A crude drawing of a flower, believed to be a water hyacinth, follows the note.

Sting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, one of us is gonna have to go home and change.

feex

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An independent anomaly-detection system under development by the government of Switzerland

This, all of it, is cool. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um… This is the 2472 slot, not the 2572 slot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aleph Room

Ye.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A new SCP based in the Dixieland Nightmare Magic canon.
Idea is a heavily modified JSoup Bright Challenge idea. Photo by Laineema, Creative Commons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After reading it a few times over, the appeal has pretty much washed off. Sure it's well written, but that's not really enough (other people like Crayne and Zyn have explained it far better than I have). I liked the nod to Aleph Room though, but that was about it. :/

Downvoted.
